title: Aortic Stenosis
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Rheumatic Heart Disease
items.content: Rheumatic fever is the leading cause of valvular heart disease in low- and middle-income countries, contributing significantly to morbidity and mortality. The prevalence varies widely based on regional factors such as overcrowding and healthcare resources.
keyPoints: Prevalence ranges from 1 per 100,000 in Costa Rica to 150 per 100,000 in China.
Higher prevalence among females aged 20–40 years.
Rheumatic heart disease accounts for 12–65% of cardiovascular hospital admissions and 2–10% of discharges in endemic countries.
~45–50 million people worldwide live with rheumatic heart disease (575.5 per 100,000).
300,000 new cases and 233,000 case fatalities (~5 per 100,000) annually.
Highest prevalence and age-adjusted mortality rates in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Central Asia, and Oceania.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence of Severe Aortic Stenosis in the Elderly
items.content: In high-income countries, degenerative processes lead to valvular heart disease, with severe aortic stenosis affecting a significant portion of those aged over 75 years. Community screenings reveal undiagnosed cases.
keyPoints: Severe aortic stenosis affects ~3.5% of the population aged >75 years.
Community echocardiographic screening identifies mild valvular heart disease in ~50% of those aged >65 years.
Prevalence of moderate or severe undiagnosed valvular heart disease is ~6% in this age group.
Significant left-sided valve disease affects 12–13% of adults aged >75 years.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Incidence and Management Challenges of Infective Endocarditis
items.content: The incidence of infective endocarditis has increased due to aging populations, more vascular grafts, intracardiac devices, and drug-resistant organisms. Valve surgery is common during acute phases.
keyPoints: North American age-standardized incidence rates for endocarditis rose from 10.1 per 100,000 in 1990 to 12.54 per 100,000 in 2019.
~50–60% of hospitalized patients with infective endocarditis undergo valve surgery during the acute phase.
Duration of intravenous antibiotic use may be shortened in selected cases.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Prevalence and Risk Factors of Calcific Aortic Stenosis in Adults
items.content: Calcific aortic stenosis (AS) is the most common valvular lesion among adults with chronic valvular heart disease. It primarily results from degenerative calcification on congenital, trileaflet, or rheumatic substrates. Key risk factors include hypertension, diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, and other traditional atherosclerotic risks.
keyPoints: AS is the most common valve lesion in adults with chronic valvular heart disease.
53% of AS cases are bicuspid, 4% unicuspid at AVR.
Risk factors: hypertension, LDL cholesterol, Lp(a), diabetes mellitus, smoking, CKD, metabolic syndrome.
Incidence of severe AS is 144 per 100,000 person-years.
Hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and dyslipidemia account for ~1/3 of population-attributable risk.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis and Progression of Calcific Aortic Stenosis
items.content: Calcific AS shares features with vascular atherosclerosis, involving endothelial dysfunction, lipid accumulation, inflammation, cytokine release, and signaling pathway upregulation. Genetic factors like vitamin D receptor polymorphisms contribute to its development. The progression from sclerosis to stenosis varies among individuals.
keyPoints: AS pathogenesis involves endothelial dysfunction, lipid accumulation, inflammatory cell activation, and cytokine release.
Genetic links: vitamin D receptor, estrogen receptor in postmenopausal women, interleukin 10, apolipoprotein E4.
Aortic valve sclerosis is prevalent in >65-year-olds (~30%) with increased cardiovascular risk.
Rheumatic AS often involves mitral valve and aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.title: Understanding Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease and Its Implications
items.content: Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is the most common congenital heart defect, affecting 0.5–1.4% of the population with a male predominance. It often leads to medial degeneration and ascending aortic aneurysm formation independent of hemodynamic severity. The genetic basis involves mutations in NOTCH1, GATA5, GATA4, endothelial nitric oxide synthase, and NKX2.5 genes.
keyPoints: BAV is the most common congenital heart valve defect (0.5–1.4% prevalence).
Male-to-female predominance of 2–4:1.
Inheritance pattern appears autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance; ~10% prevalence among first-degree relatives.
Genetic mutations include NOTCH1, GATA5, and GATA4 genes; endothelial nitric oxide synthase and NKX2.5 implicated.
Medial degeneration and ascending aortic aneurysm formation are common complications.
Aortopathy develops independently of valve lesion severity but is influenced by anatomical configuration.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.title: Differentiating Causes of Left Ventricular Outflow Tract Obstruction
items.content: Obstruction to left ventricular outflow can be caused by valvular aortic stenosis, hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, discrete fibromuscular/membranous subaortic stenosis, and supravalvular aortic stenosis. These conditions are typically differentiated using cardiac examination and Doppler echocardiography.
keyPoints: Obstruction to LV outflow can be due to valvular AS, hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, discrete fibromuscular/membranous subaortic stenosis, or supravalvular AS.
Differentiation is based on cardiac examination and Doppler echocardiographic findings.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.title: Mechanism of Adaptation and Maladaptation in Aortic Stenosis
items.content: In aortic stenosis (AS), left ventricular (LV) hypertrophy initially serves as an adaptive mechanism to maintain systolic function by reducing myocardial wall stress, according to the Laplace relation. However, excessive hypertrophy eventually becomes maladaptive, leading to reduced LV systolic function due to afterload mismatch and diastolic dysfunction.
keyPoints: Adaptive mechanism: Concentric LV hypertrophy reduces systolic wall stress (Laplace relation: $ S = Pr/h $).
Maladaptive stage: Excessive hypertrophy leads to afterload mismatch, reduced LV systolic function, and myocardial fibrosis.
Characteristic numbers: Mean systolic pressure gradient >40 mmHg or effective aortic orifice area <1 cm² (or <0.6 cm²/m²) indicates severe obstruction.

items.title: Impact of Aortic Stenosis on Coronary Blood Flow and Ischemia Risk
items.content: The hypertrophied LV in aortic stenosis increases myocardial oxygen demand and impairs coronary blood flow, particularly to the subendocardium. This can lead to ischemia under conditions of increased demand, even without obstructive coronary artery disease.
keyPoints: Increased myocardial oxygen requirements due to hypertrophied LV.
Impaired coronary blood flow: Reduced capillary density and elevated end-diastolic pressure decrease coronary driving pressure.
Subendocardium vulnerability: Especially prone to ischemia due to compressive forces and reduced coronary perfusion.

items.title: When is Aortic Stenosis Clinically Significant?
items.content: Aortic stenosis (AS) becomes clinically significant when the valve orifice narrows to approximately 1 cm². Symptoms typically manifest in the sixth to eighth decades, but earlier in patients with bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) disease.
keyPoints: Valve orifice narrowing to ~1 cm² is critical for clinical significance.
Symptoms usually appear in the sixth to eighth decades.
Patients with BAV develop symptoms one to two decades earlier.

items.title: Cardinal Symptoms and Late Manifestations of Aortic Stenosis
items.content: The three cardinal symptoms of aortic stenosis are exertional dyspnea, angina pectoris, and syncope. Late manifestations include severe pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular failure, systemic venous hypertension, hepatomegaly, atrial fibrillation, and tricuspid regurgitation.
keyPoints: Cardinal symptoms: exertional dyspnea, angina pectoris, syncope.
Late findings: severe pulmonary hypertension, RV failure, systemic venous hypertension, hepatomegaly, AF, TR.

items.title: Recognizing Aortic Stenosis through Physical Examination
items.content: Physical examination findings in aortic stenosis (AS) can provide critical insights into the severity and progression of the disease. Key signs include pulsus parvus et tardus, an accentuated a wave in the jugular venous pulse, and specific auscultatory features.
keyPoints: Pulsus parvus et tardus: Slow-rising carotid arterial pulse with delayed peak.
Accentuated a wave in jugular venous pulse due to diminished RV cavity distensibility.

Auscultation reveals an ejection systolic murmur starting shortly after S1, peaking mid-systole, and ending before aortic valve closure; loudest at the base of the heart (second right intercostal space).
Murmur characteristics: Low-pitched, rough, rasping, transmitted upward along carotid arteries.
In severe AS with preserved cardiac output, murmur is at least grade III/VI.

items.title: Auscultatory Changes in Aortic Stenosis Progression
items.content: As aortic stenosis progresses, auscultation reveals changes that reflect the severity of valve obstruction and left ventricular function. These include alterations in heart sounds and murmur characteristics.
keyPoints: Early systolic ejection sound common in congenital BAV disease; disappears with calcification.
Paradoxical splitting of S2 occurs when aortic valve closure follows pulmonic valve closure due to prolonged LV systole.
$S_3$ at apex indicates late-stage AS with LV hypertrophy and elevated end-diastolic pressure, occurring when LV dilates and function is compromised.
Murmur may be soft and brief in mild obstruction or severe stenosis with heart failure and low cardiac output.

items.title: Echocardiogram Criteria for Aortic Stenosis Severity
items.content: The echocardiogram is pivotal in assessing the severity of aortic stenosis (AS) by measuring valve area and transaortic velocity. Severe AS is characterized by a valve area less than 1 cm², while moderate and mild AS are defined by areas between 1–1.5 cm² and 1.6–2 cm², respectively.
keyPoints: Severe AS: Valve area <1 cm²
Moderate AS: Valve area 1–1.5 cm²
Mild AS: Valve area 1.6–2 cm²

items.title: Doppler Echocardiography in Low-Gradient Severe AS
items.content: In patients with severe aortic stenosis and low-flow, low-gradient conditions, dobutamine stress echocardiography is useful for assessing the severity of AS. However, it should not be used when patients have normal ejection fraction but low mean gradient (<40 mmHg) due to hypertension.
keyPoints: Useful in severe AS with low-flow, low-gradient and reduced EF
Not advised if low-gradient with normal EF and hypertension

items.title: Role of Chest CT in Uncertain Severe AS Cases
items.content: Chest computed tomography (CT) is beneficial for quantifying aortic valve calcium, aiding in the assessment of severe AS severity when echocardiographic findings are inconclusive. This is particularly relevant due to gender differences in calcification and fibrosis patterns.
keyPoints: Quantitative analysis of aortic valve calcium with CT helps clarify severity
Calcium scores differ by gender: more calcification in men, more fibrosis in women

items.title: Indications for Heart Catheterization in Aortic Stenosis
items.content: Heart catheterization is infrequently used but valuable for invasive assessment of aortic stenosis (AS), especially when there's a discrepancy between clinical and noninvasive findings. It helps define the severity of obstruction, particularly in young asymptomatic patients with congenital AS, multivalvular disease, or suspected sub- or supravalvular obstruction.
keyPoints: Useful for invasive assessment when clinical and noninvasive findings are discrepant
Risk of cerebral embolization during LV pressure measurement across the aortic valve
Indicated in patients with multivalvular disease, young asymptomatic patients with congenital AS, or suspected sub- or supravalvular obstruction
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.title: Natural History and Progression of Calcific Aortic Stenosis
items.content: Severe aortic stenosis typically leads to death in the seventh or eighth decade. Symptoms like angina, syncope, dyspnea, and heart failure precede death by 3, 3, 2, and 1.5–2 years respectively. Most patients die within four years of symptom onset. Sudden death occurs in 10-20% of symptomatic cases but is rare (~1% per year) as the first manifestation in asymptomatic adults.
keyPoints: Death commonly occurs in seventh and eighth decades
Time to death after symptoms: angina (3 years), syncope (3 years), dyspnea (2 years), heart failure (1.5–2 years)
>80% of patients die within 4 years of symptom onset
Sudden death occurs in 10-20% of symptomatic cases, ~1% per year as first manifestation in asymptomatic adults
Annual reduction in valve area: 0.1 cm²; increase in peak jet velocity and mean valve gradient: 0.3 m/s and 7 mmHg respectively
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.title: Avoidance of Strenuous Activity in Severe Aortic Stenosis
items.content: Patients with severe AS (valve area <1 cm²) should avoid strenuous physical activity and competitive sports, even if asymptomatic. This precaution helps prevent a significant reduction in cardiac output (CO). Additionally, maintaining adequate hydration is crucial to avoid hypovolemia.
keyPoints: Severe AS defined by valve area <1 cm²
Avoid strenuous activities and competitive sports
Prevent dehydration and hypovolemia to protect CO

items.title: Management of Hypertension in Aortic Stenosis
items.content: In asymptomatic patients with preserved left ventricular (LV) systolic function, medications like beta blockers and ACE inhibitors are generally safe for treating hypertension or coronary artery disease (CAD). Controlling blood pressure is crucial to mitigate the adverse effects caused by two resistance circuits (valve and arterial circulation) in series.
keyPoints: Beta blockers and ACE inhibitors are safe for asymptomatic patients with preserved LV systolic function
Control of blood pressure is important to attenuate deleterious pathophysiologic effects

items.title: Indications for Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement (SAVR) in Severe AS
items.content: Surgical intervention is recommended for patients with severe aortic stenosis (AS) who are symptomatic, exhibit left ventricular systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction <50%), or have aneurysmal root conditions. Additionally, asymptomatic patients undergoing coronary artery bypass grafting should also receive aortic valve replacement.
keyPoints: Severe AS defined by valve area <1 cm² or 0.6 cm²/m² body surface area
Symptomatic severe AS with LV systolic dysfunction (EF <50%) indicates surgery
An aneurysmal root or ascending aorta >5.5 cm in BAV disease requires operation
Aortic diameter of 4.5–5.0 cm warrants surgery for patients with family history or rapid growth (>0.5 cm/year)
Patients referred for coronary artery bypass grafting should also have AVR

items.title: Considerations for Early Surgery in Asymptomatic Severe AS
items.content: Early surgical intervention may be considered for asymptomatic patients with very severe AS, especially if they show abnormal responses to exercise or rapid progression. Indications include aortic valve jet velocity >5 m/s, mean gradient >60 mmHg, excessive LV hypertrophy without systemic hypertension, and elevated brain natriuretic peptide levels.
keyPoints: Abnormal treadmill response in asymptomatic patients may indicate early surgery
Rapid progression of AS or compromised access to medical care supports early intervention
Very severe AS defined by valve jet velocity >5 m/s or mean gradient >60 mmHg
Excessive LV hypertrophy without systemic hypertension is a relative indication for surgery
Brain natriuretic peptide level >3 times the upper reference limit with low surgical risk suggests surgery

items.title: Management of Low-Flow, Low-Gradient Severe AS
items.content: Patients with low-flow, low-gradient severe AS and reduced left ventricular ejection fraction have high perioperative mortality rates. Valve replacement is advised due to worse prognosis with medical treatment. In patients with normal LVEF, outcomes improve with surgery or TAVI compared to conservative care.
keyPoints: Low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with reduced LVEF has high perioperative mortality (15–20%)
Valve replacement is advised due to poor prognosis with medical treatment
Preoperative LV function correlates with long-term postoperative survival
Dobutamine stress echocardiography showing flow reserve supports valve replacement
In normal LVEF, surgery or TAVI improves outcomes over conservative care

items.title: Preprocedural Evaluation for AVR in Elderly Patients
items.content: Before recommending aortic valve replacement (AVR) in elderly patients with calcific aortic stenosis (AS), it is crucial to assess hepatic, renal, and pulmonary function. Age alone should not be a contraindication; instead, the patient's preoperative clinical and hemodynamic state significantly influences perioperative mortality.
keyPoints: Assessment of frailty is critical in preprocedural evaluation.
Treatment decisions for non-low operative risk AS patients involve a multidisciplinary heart team.
8-year survival rate post-isolated SAVR in older adults (mean age 74) with low surgical risk is 85–90%.

items.title: Valve Prosthesis Selection for AS
items.content: Choosing between biological and mechanical valve prostheses involves weighing the trade-offs of bioprosthetic valve durability against thromboembolism and bleeding risks associated with mechanical valves. Bioprostheses are generally favored for patients over 65, while younger patients require individualized decision-making.
keyPoints: Bioprostheses preferred for patients age >65 years.
10–20% of bioprosthetic valves fail by 15 years, requiring re-replacement or valve-in-valve TAVI.
Approximately equal percentage of mechanical prosthesis recipients develop hemorrhagic complications due to vitamin K antagonists.

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.title: Indications and Limitations of Percutaneous Aortic Balloon Valvuloplasty in Adults
items.content: Percutaneous aortic balloon valvuloplasty is not recommended as definitive therapy for adults with severe calcific AS due to high restenosis rates (80% within 1 year) and procedural complications. It may be used as a bridge to operation or TAVI in patients with severe LV dysfunction and shock.
keyPoints: High restenosis rate of 80% within 1 year in adults with severe calcific AS
Not recommended as definitive therapy for adults
May be used as a bridge to operation or TAVI in specific cases
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.title: TAVI as a Preferred Treatment for AS Across Surgical Risk Spectrum
items.content: TAVI surpassed SAVR in 2016 for treating isolated AS and is now available to symptomatic patients across all surgical risk levels due to favorable outcomes from landmark RCTs. It offers lower periprocedural mortality, stroke, major bleeding, and AF risks compared to SAVR.
keyPoints: TAVI surpassed SAVR for treating isolated AS in 2016
Available across the entire surgical risk spectrum
Lower periprocedural deaths, strokes, major bleeding, and AF compared to SAVR
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.title: Considerations for TAVI vs. SAVR in Younger Patients and Anatomical Challenges
items.content: For patients under 65 years, SAVR is recommended due to limited RCT data on TAVI outcomes. Anatomical considerations such as aortic valve/root anatomy, calcium extent, and coronary artery proximity may necessitate surgical approaches. Nonfemoral access for TAVI is associated with higher complication rates.
keyPoints: SAVR recommended for patients <65 years due to scant RCT data on TAVI
Anatomical considerations may dictate a surgical approach
Nonfemoral access for TAVI has higher complication rates
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.question: What is the most common congenital heart valve defect?
items.explanation: A bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is the most common congenital heart valve defect.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.question: Which gene mutations have been described in some families with bicuspid aortic valve disease?
items.explanation: Mutations in the NOTCH1, GATA5, and GATA4 genes have been described in some families with BAV disease.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.question: Which type of aortic abnormality is commonly associated with bicuspid aortic valve disease?
items.explanation: Medial degeneration with ascending aortic aneurysm formation occurs commonly among patients with BAV disease.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.question: What initial adaptive response does the left ventricle (LV) exhibit in response to severe aortic stenosis?
items.explanation: The initial adaptive response is concentric LV hypertrophy, which helps maintain contractile performance.

items.question: Which factor contributes to the increased myocardial oxygen requirements in aortic stenosis?
options: Reduced capillary density relative to wall thickness
items.explanation: The hypertrophied LV increases myocardial oxygen requirements, and reduced capillary density relative to wall thickness contributes to this.

items.question: What is a diagnostic challenge in late-stage aortic stenosis?
options: High-flow, high-gradient AS with normal LV function
Low-flow, low-gradient AS with reduced or normal LV systolic function
Normal coronary artery disease without obstruction
Elevated left atrial pressure with preserved ejection fraction
items.explanation: Late-stage aortic stenosis presents diagnostic challenges in the form of low-flow, low-gradient AS with reduced or normal LV systolic function.

items.question: At what degree of narrowing does aortic stenosis (AS) typically become clinically important?
items.explanation: AS becomes clinically important when the valve orifice narrows to approximately $\sim1$ cm$^2$.

items.question: Which symptom of aortic stenosis is primarily due to elevated pulmonary capillary pressure?
items.explanation: Exertional dyspnea in AS results from elevated pulmonary capillary pressure due to increased left ventricular diastolic pressures.

items.question: When is valve replacement indicated in patients with aortic stenosis?
options: Once symptoms occur or the LV ejection fraction falls below normal
Only after severe pulmonary hypertension develops
As soon as AS is diagnosed, regardless of symptoms
After the onset of atrial fibrillation
items.explanation: Valve replacement in AS is indicated once symptoms occur or if the left ventricular ejection fraction falls below normal.

items.question: Which physical finding is commonly associated with aortic stenosis (AS) in older adults?
items.explanation: Hypertension occurs commonly among older adults with AS.

items.question: What auscultatory finding may be confused with the systolic murmur of mitral regurgitation (MR) in patients with aortic stenosis?
items.explanation: The murmur may be transmitted downward and to the apex, where it may be confused with MR (Gallavardin effect).

items.question: In severe aortic stenosis with preserved cardiac output, what is the minimum grade of the murmur?
items.explanation: In almost all patients with severe obstruction and preserved CO, the murmur is at least grade III/VI.

items.question: Which echocardiogram finding is characteristic of congenitally bicuspid aortic valves?
options: Systolic opening of the aortic valve leaflets
Eccentric closure of the aortic valve cusps
Doppler indices of impaired diastolic function
items.explanation: Eccentric closure of the aortic valve cusps is characteristic of congenitally bicuspid valves.

items.question: What defines severe aortic stenosis on echocardiography?
items.explanation: Severe aortic stenosis is defined by a valve area less than 1 cm².

items.question: Why is dobutamine stress echocardiography not advised in patients with severe AS and normal EF but low mean gradient?
options: Because it increases the risk of arrhythmias
Because these patients are often hypertensive, and blood pressure should be optimized first
Because it can worsen LV systolic dysfunction
Because it is not effective in evaluating AS severity
items.explanation: Dobutamine stress echocardiography is not advised because these patients are often hypertensive, and efforts to control their systemic blood pressure should be optimized before repeating Doppler echocardiography.

items.question: What imaging method is the choice for planning transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI)?
items.explanation: Chest CT angiography has become the imaging method of choice to plan for transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI).

items.question: In which group of patients is catheterization particularly useful to define the severity of obstruction to left ventricular outflow?
options: Young, asymptomatic patients with noncalcific congenital aortic stenosis (AS)
Patients suspected of having sub- or supravalvular obstruction
items.explanation: Catheterization is particularly useful in young, asymptomatic patients with noncalcific congenital AS to define the severity of obstruction.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.question: What is a potential risk associated with attempts to cross the aortic valve for measurement of left ventricular pressures during catheterization?
items.explanation: Attempts to cross the aortic valve for measurement of LV pressures are associated with a risk of cerebral embolization.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.question: What is the average annual reduction in valve area for calcific aortic stenosis?
items.explanation: Calcific AS is characterized by an annual reduction in valve area averaging 0.1 cm².
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.question: Which medication is recommended to relieve angina pectoris in patients with coronary artery disease (CAD) and severe aortic stenosis?
items.explanation: Nitroglycerin is helpful in relieving angina pectoris in patients with CAD.

items.question: Which medications are generally safe for asymptomatic patients with severe aortic stenosis and preserved left ventricular systolic function?
options: Both beta blockers and ACE inhibitors
Neither beta blockers nor ACE inhibitors
items.explanation: Beta blockers and ACE inhibitors are generally safe for asymptomatic patients with preserved LV systolic function.

items.question: What is the role of statins in the management of severe aortic stenosis?
options: They slow the progression of AS
They are used for endocarditis prophylaxis
Their use should be based on ASCVD prevention considerations
items.explanation: The use of statin medications should be driven by considerations regarding primary and secondary prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) events.

items.question: Which patients with asymptomatic calcific aortic stenosis should be followed carefully for the development of symptoms?
options: Patients with severe obstruction and no LV dysfunction
Asymptomatic patients with calcific AS and severe obstruction
All patients regardless of AS severity
items.explanation: Asymptomatic patients with calcific AS and severe obstruction should be monitored for symptom development.

items.question: When is operation indicated in patients with aortic stenosis due to BAV disease?
options: For any aneurysmal root or ascending aorta
Only if the maximal dimension exceeds 6.0 cm
If there's an aneurysmal root or ascending aorta with a maximal dimension >5.5 cm
Operation is never indicated for BAV disease
items.explanation: Operation is indicated if AS due to BAV disease has an aneurysmal root or ascending aorta with a maximal dimension >5.5 cm.

items.question: What is the recommended action for patients with asymptomatic moderate or severe AS referred for coronary artery bypass grafting surgery?
options: Only perform coronary artery bypass grafting
Perform AVR in addition to coronary artery bypass grafting
Delay any surgical intervention until symptoms develop
Refer the patient for non-surgical management
items.explanation: Patients with asymptomatic moderate or severe AS referred for CABG should also have AVR.

items.question: What is a relative indication for surgery in asymptomatic patients with very severe AS?
options: Excessive LV hypertrophy without systemic hypertension
Brain natriuretic peptide level within normal limits
items.explanation: Excessive LV hypertrophy in the absence of systemic hypertension is a relative indication for surgery.

items.question: Which factor is NOT considered when assessing the perioperative mortality risk in patients with aortic stenosis undergoing surgical aortic valve replacement?
options: Patient's preoperative clinical and hemodynamic state
Adequacy of hepatic, renal, and pulmonary function
items.explanation: Age alone is not a contraindication to SAVR for AS.

items.question: What is the recommended valve prosthesis type generally favored for patients aged over 65 years with aortic stenosis?
items.explanation: Bioprostheses are generally favored for patients age >65 years.

items.question: What is a potential late postoperative complication of the Ross procedure?
options: Excess hazard of death over 15 years
items.explanation: Late postoperative complications include aortic root dilation.

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.question: In which patient population is percutaneous aortic balloon valvuloplasty preferred over surgical intervention?
options: Children and young adults with congenital, noncalcific AS
Adults with severe calcific AS as definitive therapy
Patients with low surgical risk undergoing TAVI
Symptomatic patients across the entire surgical risk spectrum
items.explanation: Percutaneous aortic balloon valvuloplasty is preferable in children and young adults with congenital, noncalcific AS.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.question: What is the restenosis rate within 1 year for adults with severe calcific AS after percutaneous aortic balloon valvuloplasty?
items.explanation: The restenosis rate is very high, at 80% within 1 year for adults with severe calcific AS.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.question: Which route is most commonly used for TAVI?
items.explanation: The transfemoral route is the most common approach for TAVI.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.question: Which of the following outcomes is more favorable with SAVR compared to TAVI?
items.explanation: SAVR has lower rates of permanent pacemaker use compared to TAVI.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: The incidence of infective endocarditis has decreased since 1990.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the age-standardized incidence rates for endocarditis increased from 10.1 per 100,000 population in 1990 to 12.54 per 100,000 population in 2019.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: Bicuspid aortic valve disease affects approximately 0.5–1.4% of the general population.
items.explanation: The passage states that bicuspid aortic valve disease affects as many as 0.5–1.4% of the general population.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Rheumatic aortic stenosis is commonly associated with mitral valve involvement and aortic regurgitation.
items.explanation: Rheumatic AS often involves the mitral valve and is associated with aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Aortic valve sclerosis always progresses to severe aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: While aortic valve sclerosis is associated with an increased risk, it does not always progress to severe aortic stenosis.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.statement: The inheritance pattern of bicuspid aortic valve disease is autosomal dominant with complete penetrance.
items.explanation: The inheritance pattern appears to be autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.statement: Patients with bicuspid aortic valve disease often have larger aortas than those with tricuspid aortic valve disease.
items.explanation: Patients with BAV disease have larger aortas than patients with comparable tricuspid aortic valve disease.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.statement: A mean systolic pressure gradient >40 mmHg is considered to represent severe obstruction in aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: A mean systolic pressure gradient >40 mmHg with normal cardiac output or an effective aortic orifice area <1 cm² indicates severe obstruction.

items.statement: In patients with severe AS, coronary blood flow is typically unaffected by the hypertrophied LV.
items.explanation: Coronary blood flow can be impaired in aortic stenosis due to factors like reduced capillary density and increased compressive forces.

items.statement: Patients with bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) disease typically develop significant valve dysfunction and symptoms later than those with pure AS.
items.explanation: Patients with BAV disease develop significant valve dysfunction and symptoms one to two decades sooner than those with pure or predominant AS.

items.statement: Orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and pulmonary edema are common early symptoms of aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: These symptoms occur only in the advanced stages of AS.

items.statement: The aortic valve closure sound usually precedes the pulmonic valve closure sound in severe AS.
items.explanation: In severe AS, LV systole may become prolonged so that aortic valve closure does not precede pulmonic valve closure.

items.statement: An S3 sound is typically heard early in the course of aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: An S3 generally occurs late in the course when the LV dilates and its systolic function becomes severely compromised.

items.statement: The absence of ECG signs of LV hypertrophy excludes severe aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: The absence of ECG signs of LV hypertrophy does not exclude severe obstruction.

items.statement: Aortic valve sclerosis is defined by a jet velocity greater than 2.5 m/s.
items.explanation: Aortic valve sclerosis is accompanied by a jet velocity of less than 2.5 m/s.

items.statement: Sudden death as the first manifestation of severe aortic stenosis is common in asymptomatic adult patients.
items.explanation: Sudden death as the first manifestation of severe AS is very uncommon (~1% per year) in asymptomatic adult patients.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.statement: Coronary angiography is indicated for all patients with aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: Coronary angiography is indicated to screen for CAD in appropriate patients with severe AS who are being considered for surgical or trans-catheter valve intervention.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.statement: In patients with severe AS, strenuous physical activity is recommended to maintain fitness.
items.explanation: Strenuous physical activity and competitive sports should be avoided in patients with severe AS.

items.statement: Endocarditis prophylaxis is necessary for all patients with aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: The need for endocarditis prophylaxis is restricted to AS patients with a prior history of endocarditis.

items.statement: The majority of patients with symptomatic severe AS referred for surgery are considered low risk for perioperative death or major complication.
items.explanation: ~80% of patients with symptomatic severe AS referred for surgery are considered low risk.

items.statement: In asymptomatic patients with very severe AS, early surgery does not reduce the rate of operative death or cardiovascular death compared to conservative care.
items.explanation: Early surgery in asymptomatic patients with very severe AS reduces the rate of operative death or cardiovascular death.

items.statement: Homograft AVR is typically used for patients with aortic valve endocarditis.
items.explanation: Homograft AVR is usually reserved for patients with aortic valve endocarditis.

items.statement: The Ross procedure involves the replacement of the diseased aortic valve with a mechanical prosthesis.
items.explanation: The Ross procedure involves replacing the diseased aortic valve with the autologous pulmonic valve.

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.statement: TAVI is recommended for patients under 65 years based on RCT data.
items.explanation: There are scant RCT data on TAVI outcomes in patients <65 years, so SAVR is recommended.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.statement: Nonfemoral access for TAVI is associated with lower complication rates.
items.explanation: Nonfemoral access is associated with higher complication rates.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: The prevalence of valvular heart disease increases significantly with age.
items.reason: Community echocardiographic screening identifies previously undiagnosed, predominantly mild valvular heart disease in ~50% of the population aged >65 years.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why the prevalence increases with age.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: The more restricted use of antibiotic prophylaxis since 2007 has led to an increase in incidence rates for infective endocarditis cases attributable to oropharyngeal pathogens.
items.reason: There is no convincing association between the restricted use of antibiotic prophylaxis and increased incidence rates for infective endocarditis cases due to oropharyngeal pathogens.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because there has been no convincing increase in incidence rates, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · Aortic Stenosis | GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: The process of aortic valve deterioration and calcification shares features with vascular atherosclerosis.
items.reason: This includes endothelial dysfunction, lipid accumulation, inflammatory cell activation, cytokine release, and upregulation of several signaling pathways.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · GLOBAL BURDEN OF VALVULAR HEART DISEASE (part 2) | AORTIC STENOSIS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW
items.assertion: The aortopathy in bicuspid aortic valve disease develops independently of the hemodynamic severity of the valve lesion.
items.reason: Directional shear forces dictated by the anatomic configuration of the valve influence its expression.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why the aortopathy develops independently of the hemodynamic severity.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · BICUSPID AORTIC VALVE DISEASE | OTHER FORMS OF OBSTRUCTION TO LEFT VENTRICULAR OUTFLOW

items.assertion: A double apical impulse may be appreciated in patients with aortic stenosis.
items.reason: This occurs due to the presence of an S4 and is more noticeable when the patient is in the left lateral recumbent position.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: Longitudinal strain can characterize earlier changes in LV systolic function before EF decline.
items.reason: There is robust experience with the use of longitudinal strain to detect these early changes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Chest X-ray is the best method to assess aortic valve morphology.
items.reason: Chest CT angiography has become the imaging method of choice for assessing aortic valve morphology.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as chest CT angiography is preferred.

items.assertion: Calcific aortic stenosis is a progressive disease.
items.reason: The annual reduction in valve area averages 0.1 cm², with increases in peak jet velocity and mean valve gradient.
items.explanation: Both the assertion that calcific AS is progressive and the reason provided are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · LABORATORY EXAMINATION (part 2) | NATURAL HISTORY

items.assertion: Operation should be carried out promptly after symptom onset for patients with normal-flow, high-gradient severe AS.
items.reason: Clinical decision-making is straightforward in these patients due to their condition.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why operation should be prompt.

items.assertion: Perioperative mortality rates are low in patients with low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with reduced LVEF.
items.reason: Evidence of LV dysfunction usually persists even after a technically successful operation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; perioperative mortality rates are high (15–20%) for these patients.

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.assertion: TAVI has become the preferred treatment for isolated AS in symptomatic patients across all surgical risk categories.
items.reason: This is due to favorable results observed in a series of landmark RCTs over the past decade.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains why TAVI has become preferred.
items.reference: Aortic Stenosis · PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PERCUTANEOUS AORTIC BALLOON VALVULOPLASTY | TRANSCATHETER AORTIC VALVE IMPLANTATION
items.assertion: TAVI is not suitable for all patients with severe AS.
items.reason: Some prohibitive or high surgical risk patients are not candidates due to comorbidity profile, frailty, and expected longevity.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains why TAVI is not suitable for all.
